banner: Week 26 · Prove It — Why a Theorem Isn't a Postulate
essential_question: Why must the Vertical Angles Theorem be PROVABLE from the Linear Pair Postulate, rather than standing as its own separate postulate?
objective: Justify why the Vertical Angles Theorem is a theorem (proven from the Linear Pair Postulate) rather than its own postulate, complete a short statements-reasons proof chain, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on postulates, theorems, corollaries, converses, and the named angle rules.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch two lines crossing, label ∠1–∠4, and state from memory the Linear Pair Postulate and the Vertical Angles Theorem. Then sketch parallel lines cut by a transversal and name one corresponding pair and one alternate interior pair.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 22A & 22B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 22A/22B. We check the tricky ones: postulate vs. theorem in the fill-in-the-blanks, all five converse-writing problems, and the reflexive/symmetric/transitive properties from the last three items on each page. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: the Vertical Angles Theorem is a THEOREM, not a postulate. Argue it — ∠1 and ∠2 are a linear pair (Linear Pair Postulate), so m∠1 = 180° − m∠2; ∠2 and ∠3 are ALSO a linear pair, so m∠3 = 180° − m∠2 too; both equal the same expression, so m∠1 = m∠3.

Claim 2: the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem is provable by chaining TWO earlier results together. Argue it — ∠4 ≅ ∠1 (Vertical Angles Theorem, same crossing), and ∠1 ≅ ∠5 (Corresponding Angles Postulate), so ∠4 ≅ ∠5 by the Transitive Property.

Claim 3: a postulate cannot itself be proven from something more basic within this course. Argue why that has to be true — if every statement needed a proof, you'd need an infinite chain of proofs before you could prove anything at all. Postulates are where the chain has to start.

content.preview: Next unit: two-column proofs — you'll write your first formal proof using exactly the postulates, theorems, and properties you named this week as your 'reasons' column.

success_criteria.text: I can state whether a given angle relationship is a postulate or a theorem, and name it correctly
I can justify why the Vertical Angles Theorem must be provable from the Linear Pair Postulate rather than being its own separate postulate
I can write the converse of a postulate or theorem, apply the Reflexive/Symmetric/Transitive properties, and complete a short statements-reasons proof chain

questions.prompt: The statement 'a linear pair is supplementary' is treated in this course as a…
questions.explain: The Linear Pair Postulate is accepted outright — it's what a straight angle IS, not something proven from an earlier fact.

questions.prompt: 'Vertical angles are congruent' is a ___, because it is proven from the Linear Pair Postulate rather than assumed outright.
questions.explain: A theorem is proven from postulates and earlier theorems — the Vertical Angles Theorem is built entirely from the Linear Pair Postulate.

questions.prompt: Prove it: use the Linear Pair Postulate to explain why m∠1 = m∠3 whenever two lines cross at a point, forming ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 in order.
questions.answer: ∠1 and ∠2 are a linear pair, so m∠1 + m∠2 = 180°, meaning m∠1 = 180° − m∠2. ∠2 and ∠3 are also a linear pair, so m∠3 = 180° − m∠2 too. Since both equal the same expression, m∠1 = m∠3.
questions.explain: Both angles are shown equal to '180° minus m∠2' — the Transitive Property then forces them equal to each other.

questions.prompt: Complete the proof. Given: ∠1 and ∠2 form a linear pair; ∠2 and ∠3 form a linear pair. Prove: ∠1 ≅ ∠3. Statements: (1) ∠1 & ∠2 are a linear pair, ∠2 & ∠3 are a linear pair. (2) m∠1 + m∠2 = 180°, m∠2 + m∠3 = 180°. (3) m∠1 + m∠2 = m∠2 + m∠3. (4) m∠1 = m∠3. (5) ∠1 ≅ ∠3. Fill in the REASON for each of the five statements above.
questions.answer: (1) Given. (2) Linear Pair Postulate. (3) Substitution — both sums equal 180°. (4) Subtraction Property of Equality — subtract m∠2 from both sides. (5) Definition of congruent angles. This chain is exactly the proof of the Vertical Angles Theorem.
questions.explain: This IS the Vertical Angles Theorem, proven — not assumed — using only the Linear Pair Postulate and basic properties of equality.

questions.prompt: Write the CONVERSE of: 'If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then corresponding angles are congruent.' Is the converse also true in this course?
questions.answer: Converse: if corresponding angles are congruent, then the two lines cut by the transversal are parallel. Yes — this converse is also true; it's how surveyors and framers prove two lines are parallel just by measuring one pair of corresponding angles.
questions.explain: Swap the hypothesis and conclusion to form the converse; MUS flags this specific list of statements as having true converses, which isn't guaranteed for statements in general.

questions.prompt: Name the property of equality/congruence that justifies each: (a) ∠A ≅ ∠A. (b) If ∠A ≅ ∠B, then ∠B ≅ ∠A. (c) If ∠A ≅ ∠B and ∠B ≅ ∠C, then ∠A ≅ ∠C.
questions.answer: (a) Reflexive Property. (b) Symmetric Property. (c) Transitive Property.
questions.explain: Reflexive = itself; Symmetric = flip the order; Transitive = chain through a shared middle term.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 22 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one postulate or theorem name you'd want to drill more before starting two-column proofs next.
